records.ety: [OE. deinen, deignen, OF. degner, deigner, daigner, F. daigner, fr. L. dignari to deem worthy, deign, fr. dignus worthy; akin to decere to be fitting. See Decent, and cf. Dainty, Dignity, Condign, Disdain.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Deigned (dānd); p. pr. & vb. n. Deigning.]

defs: To esteem worthy; to consider worth notice; -- opposed to disdain.
quotes: I fear my Julia would not deign my lines.

defs: To condescend to give or bestow; to stoop to furnish; to vouchsafe; to allow; to grant.
quotes: Nor would we deign him burial of his men.

defs: To think worthy; to vouchsafe; to condescend; - - followed by an infinitive.
quotes: O deign to visit our forsaken seats.
Yet not Lord Cranstone deigned she greet.
Round turned he, as not deigning Those craven ranks to see.
Him deyneth not to set his foot to ground.
notes: In early English deign was often used impersonally.
